front: Film: Peter Lorre plays Hans Beckert, a compulsive child murderer who whistles Grieg's 'In the Hall of the Mountain King'; a blind balloon vendor identifies him by the tune; he is marked with the letter 'M' in chalk on his overcoat
Film: at his trial in an abandoned distillery, the child murderer cries 'I cannot help myself! I have no control over this evil thing inside me — the fire, the voices, the torment!'
Film: ends with three women in black mourning, one speaking a warning to an unseen audience; the murderer's shadow appears on a girl's wanted poster at the opening
Film: evil scientist Rotwang transfers the likeness of labor leader Maria onto a robot to incite riots; protagonist Freder hallucinates workers being fed into the machine Moloch
Film: the Schufftan process (mirrors + miniatures) was invented for this film; lost reels were found in 2008 in Buenos Aires; inter-title reads 'the mediator between head and hands must be the heart'

Film: villainous hypnotist Dr. Mabuse trilogy — 'a Gambler' (1922, stock market panic; Countess Told seduced), 'Testament' (1933, criminal plans dictated from asylum), 'Thousand Eyes' (1960); opening of first film inspired by Feuillade's Fantomas
Film: Lee Marvin throws boiling coffee in Gloria Grahame's face; fired detective Dave Bannion hunts his wife's killers; a woman coolly calls police saying 'this is Tom Duncan's widow'

Film: a mob tries to lynch a mechanic played by Spencer Tracy; MGM forced the director to cast a white lead; a woman stumbles through a crowd staring at a burning building; protagonist holds a dog named Rainbow
Film: contains the first onscreen countdown to a rocket launch; rocket scientist Hermann Oberth was a technical consultant; screenplay by Thea von Harbou

Director: fled Germany after allegedly declining an offer to head the Nazi film division; later made the anti-Nazi Hangmen Also Die! about the assassination of Reinhard Heydrich; wife and screenwriter was Thea von Harbou
Director: appeared as himself directing a film of the Odyssey in Jean-Luc Godard's Contempt (1963); producers forced Godard to add a nude scene with Brigitte Bardot to that film
